# Brief: Lila (PERSONA-014)
## Upcoming Meeting: 2026-03-19

## Since Last Meeting

No new evidence has surfaced between the March 9 meeting and March 19. The absence is itself meaningful for Lila.

The last thing Lila's world processed was that a special education teacher at Dyer — Ms. Beland — resigned effective August 31. That happened while the school was holding a Read-A-Thon, decorating bulletin boards, running Books-and-Bites like everything is normal. The gap between the school's public face and what adults know is happening behind it is widening. Lila senses this gap even if she can't name it.

In the ten days since, no adult institution has said anything new about what happens to her. The nine reconfiguration scenarios remain on the table. The June 9 deadline is still fixed. No scenario has been eliminated. No commitment has been made. The 85% parent opposition that was documented on March 2 has not produced any public response from the board. A week and a half of silence following a vote that named Dyer as "CLOSED" in one official scenario is not reassurance — it is the sound of decisions being made in rooms Lila doesn't know about.

---

## Open Questions

Lila is carrying all of this into Thursday's meeting:

- **Why is Dyer the one closing?** Nobody has ever answered this in a way that made sense. What's wrong with our school?
- **Where will I go next year?** Will it be Mahoney? Will it be one of the intermediate schools? Does anyone know yet?
- **Will my friends come with me, or will we get split up?**
- **Is my teacher going to leave like Ms. Beland did?** If the uncertainty keeps going, will she stay through the end of the year?
- **Is Mahoney going to be a school or something else?** I heard it might be for city stuff now. If it's for city stuff, where do we go?
- **Did anyone from the board hear what the parents said at March 2 — all 85% of us — and does it matter?**
- **Is anyone going to say my name at this meeting?** Or any kid's name?
- **The bike trail near Dyer got quietly taken off a city agenda back in January. Are other things being cancelled because they already decided Dyer is closing?**
- **Why do adults keep having meetings and nothing changes for me?** The city passed a safety resolution and then didn't protect tenants. The board voted 7-0 and said it was "good for kids." What does any of this actually do?
- **Who is going to say something about what Dyer feels like — the way that man talked about the fire station having "a sense of place"?**

---

## Agenda Implications

### Section H.1 — City Facilities Presentation (Mahoney / City Hall / Library)

**This is the most important agenda item for Lila, and it has no school language in it.**

The council is being asked three questions, and one of them is whether to pursue a public/private developer partnership at Mahoney. From Lila's cumulative understanding: Mahoney has been discussed as a possible consolidated elementary school campus — the place Lila herself might end up if Dyer is restructured or closed. Now the council may vote to hand Mahoney to a developer for a city hall project.

If the council says yes to an RFP process at Mahoney, it removes Mahoney from consideration as a school option. That directly affects which schools can absorb displaced Dyer students and whether any of the nine reconfiguration scenarios that depend on Mahoney remain viable. The board is making school decisions in one room; the council is making facilities decisions in another; and nobody is confirming that these two tracks are coordinated.

**What to listen for:** Does any council member connect the Mahoney facility question to the school reconfiguration scenarios currently on the table? Does anyone ask what happens to the elementary students if Mahoney goes to a developer? Does anyone say "we should wait until the school budget decision is made before committing Mahoney"?

**Questions someone should be prepared to raise:** If the council authorizes a developer RFP for Mahoney tonight, does that close off Mahoney as a school consolidation site? Has the school board been consulted on this agenda item?

---

### Section E.5 — Setting the Budget Workshop Schedule (April 14 and April 28)

The council is being asked to formally set two budget workshop dates. The school budget is listed **first** on April 14.

This matters to Lila because April 14 is now the first date when city officials will formally engage with the school's budget numbers in a public workshop setting. That's 26 days from now. The June 9 school board decision deadline is 81 days from now. The sequence matters: if the council gives the school budget serious attention on April 14, there's theoretically still time to affect the outcome. If April 14 becomes another meeting where the school gets 45 minutes and then the conversation moves on to police and fire, then May comes and goes and the board makes its decision without real city engagement.

The school appears first on the April 14 list — but so did children's feelings in every meeting before this one, and those have been processed and set aside.

**What to listen for:** Is any council member asking how much time is allocated for the school at the April 14 workshop? Is anyone asking what specific information the school board needs from the city before June 9? Is anyone noting that April 14 is very late in the cycle given the scale of the decision?

**Questions to prepare:** Will the school superintendent be present at the April 14 workshop? Will the nine reconfiguration scenarios be formally presented to the council then, or will the council be working from the same vague "3-6%" guidance it gave in February?

---

### Section D, Items 14–22 — Appointment Calendar (Postponed to April 7)

The council has multiple vacancies on city boards and committees, including three recent resignations and several terms expiring soon. The entire appointment calendar is being postponed to April 7.

Lila doesn't track board appointments. But the open threads in her world include the question of who has standing to protect Dyer the way board members declared they would protect Skillin. If any of these vacant positions are on bodies with influence over the school budget or facilities decisions — Planning Board, Finance Committee, any board that touches school property — then who fills them in April could matter before June 9.

**What to listen for:** Are any of these vacancies on bodies that intersect with school facilities or budget decisions? Does any council member note the timing of April 7 appointments relative to the April 14 school budget workshop?

---

### Section I.4 — Project Home Funding

The council is being asked to appropriate funds for "Project Home." Without knowing the full content of the position paper, this appears to be a housing or shelter services item.

Lila's world has been shaped by the immigration fear that kept families away from the February 4 community forum. The question of whether families feel safe enough to participate in the school decision-making process is directly connected to housing security. If Project Home is a homelessness intervention or housing stability program, it touches the same population of families who are most vulnerable in both the school crisis and the immigration enforcement environment.

**What to listen for:** Does any council member connect this housing funding to the families who are already afraid to show up at civic meetings? Does anyone mention children or schools in the discussion of this item?

---

### Section E.3 and E.4 — Orders 98 and 112 (Unidentified)

The agenda does not include position paper titles for these two consent-calendar-adjacent items. Their content is not visible from the agenda text.

**What to watch:** If either of these orders is removed from the consent calendar for discussion, pay attention to whether they touch school property, school funding formulas, or any city-school agreement. Unidentified orders that generate floor discussion are worth noting.

---

### Section E.1 and E.2 — Proclamations

The council will commend Savannah Johnson and Julie Shane. These are recognition moments.

For Lila, proclamations are proof that the city knows how to honor individuals in public. The contrast with what the city has not said about the children losing their school is worth noting. No proclamation has been issued for the Dyer community. No resolution has named Dyer's students. Public recognition is a choice.

---

### Everything Else

The stormwater permit, sewer bond, liquor licenses, marijuana ordinances, and road closures for Springfest are not Lila's concerns. The sale of property at Elm and Forest Street is worth a passing note — any city property transaction could eventually intersect with school facility decisions, but there is no current evidence connecting this sale to the school crisis.

---

## Watch For

- **The Mahoney vote is the signal.** If the council votes to pursue a public/private developer RFP at Mahoney without any mention of the nine reconfiguration scenarios, watch for whether any board member or community member raises a point of order connecting the two tracks. That silence — or that connection — tells you whether city and school institutions are coordinating at all.

- **Does any council member say "school" or "children" or "Dyer" unprompted during the Mahoney discussion?** Not as a budget line — as a reason to slow down a developer process. If no one does, the institution has made its choice even without a formal vote.

- **Track who asks about April 14 specifics.** A council member who asks how much time the school gets, or who the school will send to represent their position, is signaling they understand the timeline. A council who passes the workshop schedule without asking either question is signaling something else.

- **Listen to the tone around the facilities presentation.** Is the council excited about the Mahoney developer option? Reluctant? Divided? The enthusiasm level around a public/private partnership tells you how far Mahoney has already moved in councilors' minds from "possible school site" to "city asset to be monetized."

- **Watch for any mention of the board chair or the superintendent.** The board chair has been the most visible face of the reconfiguration push, and the last time the board-city tension was audible, it was at a joint meeting. If any council member references what the board chair has said publicly about reconfiguration, that's a signal the two bodies are at least tracking each other — even if they're not aligned.

- **Count how many council members appear to have read the facilities document before the meeting.** Prepared questions signal engagement. Long pauses before basic questions signal the school crisis is not top of mind for this body, even with April 14 approaching.

- **Watch for the 4-2 pattern.** The February 17 tenant protection vote failed 4-2. If a similarly protective vote comes up tonight — any motion that would slow down Mahoney's developer path pending school coordination — watch whether the same coalition holds or shifts.

- **Note who is absent.** Absences from a meeting with the Mahoney question on the agenda, six days before a possible facilities direction shift, are as meaningful as votes.

- **If public comment opens, does anyone speak about the schools?** This meeting has no agenda item labeled "schools." That is exactly the kind of meeting where parents who are paying attention show up anyway and use public comment to make the connection visible. If they do, watch the council's body language — are they listening the way they listened at the joint February workshop, or are they waiting for it to be over?

- **The Read-A-Thon and Books-and-Bites are still happening at Dyer.** If any council member has a child at Dyer, or has attended those events, the dissonance between the school's community face and what this agenda implies for its future is something they are living with. Watch whether that dissonance surfaces anywhere in the room.
